abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based evolution is a promising approach for open-ended discovery, where progress requires sustained search and knowledge accumulation. Existing methods still rely heavily on fixed heuristics and hard-coded exploration rules, which limit the autonomy of LLM agents. We present CORAL, the first framework for autonomous multi-agent evolution on open-ended problems. CORAL replaces rigid control with long-running agents that explore, reflect, and collaborate through shared persistent memory, asynchronous multi-agent execution, and heartbeat-based interventions. It also provides practical safeguards, including isolated workspaces, evaluator separation, resource management, and agent session and health management. Evaluated on diverse mathematical, algorithmic, and systems optimization tasks, CORAL sets new state-of-the-art results on 10 tasks, achieving 3-10 times higher improvement rates with far fewer evaluations than fixed evolutionary search baselines across tasks. On Anthropic's kernel engineering task, four co-evolving agents improve the best known score from 1363 to 1103 cycles. Mechanistic analyses further show how these gains arise from knowledge reuse and multi-agent exploration and communication. Together, these results suggest that greater agent autonomy and multi-agent evolution can substantially improve open-ended discovery. Code is available at this https URL.

citations.fullCitation: Bernardino Romera-Paredes, Mohammadamin Barekatain, Alexander Novikov, Matej Balog, M. Pawan Kumar, Emilien Dupont, Francisco J. R. Ruiz, Jordan S. Ellenberg, Pengming Wang, Omar Fawzi, Pushmeet Kohli, and Alhussein Fawzi. Mathematical discoveries from program search with large language models. Nature, 625(7995):468–475, 2024. doi: 10.1038/s41586-023-06924-6.
citations.justification: This paper, which introduced FunSearch, is cited as the foundational work that established the paradigm of using LLMs within an evolutionary loop for open-ended discovery. CORAL's central thesis is to improve upon this 'fixed evolutionary search' model by granting agents greater autonomy, making this citation essential for context and contrast.
citations.title: Mathematical discoveries from program search with large language models

citations.fullCitation: Alexander Novikov, Ng ˆan V ˜u, Marvin Eisenberger, Emilien Dupont, Po-Sen Huang, Adam Zsolt Wagner, Sergey Shirobokov, Borislav Kozlovskii, Francisco JR Ruiz, Abbas Mehrabian, et al. Alphaevolve: A coding agent for scientific and algorithmic discovery. arXiv preprint arXiv:2506.13131, 2025.
citations.justification: AlphaEvolve is presented as a significant extension of the FunSearch paradigm, applying it to full codebases. It serves as a prime example of the state-of-the-art fixed evolutionary search systems that CORAL directly compares against and aims to surpass by introducing agent autonomy.
citations.title: Alphaevolve: A coding agent for scientific and algorithmic discovery

citations.fullCitation: Shu Liu, Shubham Agarwal, Monishwaran Maheswaran, Mert Cemri, Zhifei Li, Qiuyang Mang, Ashwin Naren, Ethan Boneh, Audrey Cheng, Melissa Z. Pan, Alexander Du, Kurt Keutzer, Alvin Cheung, Alexandros G. Dimakis, Koushik Sen, Matei Zaharia, and Ion Stoica. EvoX: Meta-evolution for automated discovery. arXiv preprint arXiv:2602.23413, 2026a.
citations.justification: EvoX is used as a primary experimental baseline and is explicitly identified in the paper as the 'strongest competitor' among existing fixed evolutionary search methods. This direct comparison is crucial for demonstrating the performance benefits of CORAL's autonomous, multi-agent framework over the previous state-of-the-art.
citations.title: EvoX: Meta-evolution for automated discovery

citations.fullCitation: Noah Shinn, Federico Cassano, Ashwin Gopinath, Karthik Narasimhan, and Shunyu Yao. Reflexion: Language agents with verbal reinforcement learning. In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, volume 36, 2023.
citations.justification: This work is cited as a key inspiration from the autonomous LLM agent literature, specifically for its use of self-feedback and reflection. CORAL incorporates this concept into its design through its 'heartbeat' mechanism, which prompts agents to reflect and consolidate knowledge, thus bridging the gap between autonomous agent capabilities and evolutionary search.
citations.title: Reflexion: Language agents with verbal reinforcement learning

intermediateReport:
The following report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "CORAL: Towards Autonomous Multi-Agent Evolution for Open-Ended Discovery."

---

## Research Report: CORAL: Towards Autonomous Multi-Agent Evolution for Open-Ended Discovery

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by a collaborative team of authors from various institutions:

*   **Ao Qu, Han Zheng, Kaichen Zhou, Chonghe Jiang, Cathy Wu, Jinhua Zhao, Paul Pu Liang**: Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and Singapore-MIT Alliance for Research and Technology (SMART).
*   **Zijian Zhou, Shao Yong Ong, Bryan Kian Hsiang Low**: National University of Singapore (NUS) and Singapore-MIT Alliance for Research and Technology (SMART). Zijian Zhou is also affiliated with MiniMax.
*   **Yihao Yan**: McGill University.
*   **Yihong Tang**: Stanford University and SambaNova.
*   **Minwei Kong**: Singapore-MIT Alliance for Research and Technology (SMART).
*   **Jiacheng Zhu**: Meta.
*   **Xuan Jiang**: Amazon.
*   **Sirui Li**: Microsoft.

The authors with an asterisk (*) are noted as equal contributors, while those with a dagger (†) indicate joint advising. Authors with a double dagger (‡) performed the work outside their primary employment.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The work presented in this paper is positioned within the evolving field of Large Language Model (LLM)-powered agents for complex problem-solving, specifically focusing on open-ended discovery tasks.

Prior research has demonstrated the effectiveness of LLMs embedded in evaluator-guided evolutionary search loops. Systems such as FunSearch and AlphaEvolve operate by having an LLM propose candidate programs based on high-scoring solutions from a population. External evaluators then execute and score these candidates, and a predetermined evolutionary algorithm (e.g., MAP-Elites, island models, Pareto-based selection) dictates parent selection and population updates. This approach has shown success in areas like mathematical discovery, algorithm design, and systems optimization.

However, the authors identify limitations in this fixed evolutionary search paradigm. Key decisions within the search process—such as which parent solutions to inspect, when to run intermediate tests, and what knowledge to externalize for future reuse—are governed by fixed heuristics and external rules, rather than by the LLM agents themselves. This rigidity limits the autonomy of the LLM agents, potentially hindering their ability to adapt and explore effectively in complex, open-ended problem spaces where optimal search strategies are not known a priori.

Furthermore, existing multi-agent LLM systems often rely on "vertical scaling," where human operators decompose tasks, assign specialized roles, and define rigid communication structures. While effective for certain problems, this approach presumes prior knowledge of the optimal task decomposition and interaction topology, which is restrictive for truly open-ended discovery.

CORAL seeks to advance this landscape by introducing a framework that shifts decision-making autonomy to the agents themselves and enables "horizontal parallelism" through asynchronous multi-agent coordination. It aims to move beyond fixed evolutionary search and structured multi-agent collaboration towards a more autonomous, self-organizing evolutionary paradigm. This positions CORAL as an attempt to enhance the flexibility, adaptability, and scalability of LLM-based discovery systems by granting agents greater agency over their evolutionary process and fostering indirect, emergent collaboration.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to develop and evaluate a framework, named CORAL, that enables autonomous multi-agent evolution for open-ended discovery problems. This objective is driven by two main motivations arising from the limitations of current LLM-based evolutionary approaches:

1.  **Addressing the Rigidity of Fixed Evolutionary Search**: Many scientific and engineering problems lack ground-truth answers, requiring continuous iterative discovery through proposal, testing, and revision. Current LLM-based evolutionary methods, while effective, largely rely on fixed evolutionary search algorithms. In this paradigm, critical decisions—such as selecting parent solutions, determining when to conduct intermediate tests, and deciding what knowledge to record and reuse—are pre-defined and external to the LLM agent. The LLM primarily acts as a mutation operator within a scaffolded loop. The authors hypothesize that for challenging open-ended problems, these choices are integral to the evolutionary process itself. The motivation is to investigate whether delegating a greater portion of the evolutionary algorithm's control to autonomous agents can lead to stronger performance by allowing agents to make dynamic, intelligent search decisions based on their analysis of prior attempts and feedback.

2.  **Enabling Scalable Multi-Agent Collaboration for Open-Ended Problems**: Existing multi-agent LLM systems often scale "vertically," where human designers establish specialized roles and fixed communication structures. This approach assumes that the optimal task decomposition and interaction patterns are known in advance. However, for open-ended problems, such assumptions may not hold. The authors are motivated to explore whether multiple autonomous agents can achieve more effective "horizontal parallelism" by independently exploring solution spaces, asynchronously exchanging discoveries, and building upon each other's progress without requiring pre-defined roles or explicit communication protocols. The aim is to understand if emergent collaboration through shared knowledge can outperform structured, predefined multi-agent interactions in sustained discovery.

In essence, CORAL's objective is to shift the paradigm of LLM-based discovery from rigid, externally-controlled search to a more flexible, agent-driven process where individual and collective intelligence guides continuous optimization and knowledge accumulation over long horizons.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

CORAL proposes a framework for autonomous multi-agent evolution by empowering LLM agents with greater control over the evolutionary loop and facilitating indirect coordination. The methodology builds upon a general problem formulation for open-ended discovery, where an optimal solution is unknown and improvement occurs through iterative search guided by an evaluator `E(x, y)` yielding a score `s` and feedback `f`. The abstract improvement step involves four stages: RETRIEVE, PROPOSE, EVALUATE, and UPDATE.

CORAL distinguishes itself from "fixed evolutionary search" (where these stages are governed by external rules) and "autonomous single-agent evolution" (where a single agent controls more stages) by realizing "autonomous multi-agent evolution."

**Key Architectural Features:**

The CORAL framework incorporates three core mechanisms and several practical safeguards to enable this paradigm:

1.  **Shared Persistent Memory as a File System:**
    *   **Structure:** Memory (`M`) is implemented as a file system, with symbolic links connecting it to each agent's isolated workspace. This design ensures consistency and allows agents to interact with shared knowledge using standard file system operations.
    *   **Interaction:** Agents use a `CORAL CLI` tool (and standard Bash) to retrieve from and contribute to the shared file system.
    *   **Organization:** Knowledge is categorized into three root folders to provide initial structure:
        *   `attempts/`: Stores historical candidate solutions and their evaluation outcomes. Agents can inspect these to understand high-performing solutions.
        *   `notes/`: Contains markdown files recording observations, learnings, and reflections from all agents. This serves as a collective knowledge base.
        *   `skills/`: Houses reusable procedures, tools, scripts, and implementation patterns. Each skill includes a natural-language description and executable artifacts. Agents are provided with a "skill creator" skill to guide new skill development.

2.  **Asynchronous Multi-Agent Organization:**
    *   **Parallelism:** A population of `N` agents runs asynchronously, each maintaining its own local context (`C(i)t`) within an isolated workspace.
    *   **Indirect Coordination:** Agents coordinate primarily through the shared persistent memory. There are no predefined roles or explicit messaging protocols. As agents generate `attempts`, `notes`, and `skills`, they write these artifacts (`W(i)t`) to the shared memory `M`. Other agents can then retrieve these artifacts as part of their own context (`M(j)t`), allowing discoveries to diffuse organically and influence future search directions.
    *   **Benefits:** This organization promotes exploration diversity, as agents can pursue different local optima in parallel while still benefiting from collective knowledge accumulation.

3.  **Heartbeat-based Interventions:**
    *   **Purpose:** To prevent agents from getting stuck in local minima, encourage consultation and contribution to shared memory, and reorient search when progress plateaus.
    *   **Triggers:** Heartbeat events can be predefined (e.g., number of intervals, time passed) or agent-created, and are tied to specific conditions (e.g., lack of improvement).
    *   **Mechanism:** When triggered, a heartbeat applies a modification to an agent's local context (`C(i)t → C'(i)t`) through structured prompts, thereby steering its subsequent behavior.
    *   **Types:**
        *   **Per-iteration reflection heartbeat:** Prompts agents to record useful notes during ongoing work, capturing immediate observations.
        *   **Periodic consolidation heartbeat:** Triggered after a fixed number of attempts, prompting agents to review progress, organize notes, and distill reusable procedures into skills.
        *   **Stagnation-triggered redirection heartbeat:** Activated when an agent shows no improvement for several rounds, prompting it to reassess its current direction, revise its strategy, or pivot to a new line of search.

**Practical Safeguards:**

CORAL also integrates several practical safeguards to ensure robust and safe operation:
*   **Isolated Workspaces:** Each agent operates in a dedicated Git worktree, preventing direct interference between agents' codebases.
*   **Evaluator Separation:** The grader implementation is hidden from agents (located in `.coral/private/eval/grader.py`), preventing reward hacking.
*   **Resource Management:** Includes mechanisms for process management, such as tracking agent PIDs and implementing graceful shutdown procedures.
*   **Agent Session and Health Management:** The system monitors agent processes, automatically restarts crashed agents, and preserves agent sessions for resume functionality.
*   **Evaluation Timeout:** Grader invocations run in child processes with a configurable hard timeout to prevent indefinite execution.

The system exposes its functionality to agents through a `coral CLI` (e.g., `coral eval`, `coral log`, `coral show`, `coral notes`, `coral skills`) and provides a web-based dashboard for real-time monitoring of agent activities and performance.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The experiments evaluated CORAL against fixed evolutionary search baselines on diverse tasks and assessed the contributions of multi-agent collaboration and specific mechanisms.

**5.1. Autonomous Evolution Outperforms Fixed Evolutionary Search (Single-Agent)**

*   **Performance Metrics:** On a suite of 6 mathematical optimization tasks and 5 systems optimization tasks, a single autonomous CORAL agent achieved the best final score on all 11 tasks. It established new state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on 8 of these tasks.
*   **Efficiency:** CORAL exhibited a 3 to 10 times higher improvement rate (fraction of evaluations yielding an improvement) compared to baselines (OpenEvolve, ShinkaEvolve, EvoX). It typically converged to its final score within 5 to 20 evaluations, while fixed evolutionary search methods required 60 to 100 evaluations. This indicates more efficient use of computational resources and LLM calls.
*   **Comparison to Baselines:** CORAL consistently outperformed EvoX, which was identified as the strongest competitor among the fixed evolutionary search baselines due to its meta-evolved search strategy.

**5.2. Multi-Agent Evolution Extends the Search Frontier**

*   **Stress-Test Performance:** On challenging stress-test problems, multi-agent CORAL (4 agents) pushed performance beyond the single-agent CORAL results.
    *   **Kernel Engineering:** Achieved an 18.3% cycle reduction, improving the best known score from 1363 cycles (previous SOTA) to 1103 cycles. This represents a new SOTA.
    *   **Polyominoes Packing:** Showed a 5.0% score increase. With web search enabled, CORAL achieved 89.4% coverage, surpassing the previous SOTA of 87%.
*   **Mechanism of Gain:** The improvements are attributed to CORAL’s asynchronous shared persistent memory, which enables multiple agents to explore diverse solution spaces in parallel and share discoveries organically. Useful techniques diffuse across agents without requiring explicit coordination protocols.
*   **Generalization to Open-Source Models:** The multi-agent performance gains were observed not only with proprietary models (Claude Opus 4.6) but also with an open-source stack (MiniMax M2.5 + OpenCode) on the math and systems suites. This indicates that CORAL's organizational advantages stem from the co-evolution mechanism itself rather than being specific to particular LLM capabilities.

**5.3. Analysis of Autonomous and Multi-Agent Mechanisms**

*   **Why Autonomous Evolution Works:**
    *   **Local Verification:** Agents frequently execute code and run tests locally before external evaluation. This local testing was associated with a higher improvement rate (e.g., 61% local test rate on Transaction, 57% on Kernel Engineering), indicating effectiveness in debugging and validating candidates before consuming an external evaluation budget.
    *   **Knowledge Accumulation:** The creation and access of notes and skills correlated with performance. On advanced tasks like Kernel Engineering and Polyominoes, agents created significantly more knowledge artifacts per attempt (0.55-0.68 vs. 0.05 on standard tasks) and knowledge access was strongly associated with improvement (55% on Kernel Engineering vs. 26% on standard tasks). This knowledge on advanced tasks included reusable insights and documentation of failed approaches.
*   **Why Multi-Agent Organization Helps:**
    *   **Cross-Agent Information Transfer:** Agents building on another agent's work showed higher improvement rates. For instance, on Kernel Engineering, 36% of attempts used a cross-agent parent, yielding a 17% improvement rate compared to 9% for all attempts, with 66% of new records originating from cross-agent parents. On Polyominoes, knowledge transfer occurred more through shared notes and skills (87% of rounds referenced other agents' knowledge).
    *   **Exploration Diversity:** Analysis of strategy keywords showed low pairwise Jaccard similarity (0.43 on Kernel Engineering, 0.31 on Polyominoes), indicating that the agent population collectively explored a substantially larger portion of the search space than any single agent.
    *   **Contribution Balance:** On Kernel Engineering, contributions across agents were relatively balanced, with all four agents contributing attempts and improvements and independently reaching the best score. On Polyominoes, contributions were less balanced, but overall collective progress was still evident.

**5.4. Ablation Studies**

*   **Knowledge Accumulation:** Disabling the creation of notes and skills consistently degraded final scores across all three stress-test tasks. On Kernel Engineering, this resulted in an 18.6% regression in cycles (from 1350 to 1601), confirming the causal role of knowledge artifacts in search quality.
*   **Co-evolution vs. Independent Runs:** Comparing 4-agent co-evolution against the best performance from four independent single-agent runs revealed that co-evolution consistently outperformed the independent best. This result indicates that the gains from multi-agent evolution are not merely a function of increased computational resources but arise from the synergistic interactions and shared knowledge accumulation.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The CORAL framework and its empirical results demonstrate that granting greater autonomy to LLM agents within an evolutionary search paradigm can substantially enhance performance on open-ended discovery problems. The shift from fixed heuristics to agent-controlled decision-making regarding retrieval, proposal, evaluation, and knowledge accumulation represents a notable advancement.

The work highlights several significant implications:

1.  **New Paradigm for Open-Ended Discovery**: CORAL establishes "autonomous evolution" as a distinct and effective paradigm, moving beyond prior fixed evolutionary search methods. This suggests a more flexible and adaptive approach to problems where optimal solutions are unknown and continuous progress is required.
2.  **Effectiveness of Multi-Agent Collaboration**: The findings indicate that asynchronous multi-agent evolution, coordinated primarily through shared persistent memory, can extend the search frontier beyond what single autonomous agents can achieve, even with equivalent computational resources. This demonstrates the power of emergent collaboration and knowledge diffusion in complex problem spaces.
3.  **Efficiency and Robustness**: CORAL's higher improvement rates and lower number of evaluations suggest a more efficient use of LLM resources. The mechanistic analyses underscore the importance of local verification and systematic knowledge accumulation (through notes and skills) in driving successful discovery.
4.  **Generality and Extensibility**: The framework's ability to achieve gains with both proprietary and open-source LLMs suggests that its organizational advantages are generalizable. The design provides an extensible infrastructure for future research into autonomous discovery systems.

The potential impact of CORAL includes advancing scientific and engineering discovery by enabling AI systems to more effectively tackle complex, iteratively solvable problems. This approach could lead to the automated discovery of more efficient algorithms, novel mathematical solutions, and optimized system designs. The authors also acknowledge that this progress, while exciting, raises important challenges for the research community, implying considerations for responsible development and deployment of increasingly autonomous AI systems. The paper positions CORAL as a systematic exploratory study, a strong baseline framework, and an extensible infrastructure to support future work in this area.
overview:
In the landscape of modern artificial intelligence, solving complex problems often involves iterative refinement. For tasks where the optimal solution is unknown—such as optimizing high-performance code or discovering new mathematical proofs—researchers typically rely on evolutionary search. Historically, these search processes have been "fixed," meaning a human designer dictates the rules for how solutions are proposed, evaluated, and updated. The CORAL framework represents a shift in this paradigm by delegating the control of the evolutionary process to autonomous Large Language Model (LLM) agents. By allowing multiple agents to coordinate asynchronously through shared knowledge, CORAL enables a form of "horizontal parallelism" that allows AI to explore and discover solutions in open-ended domains more effectively than traditional, rigid search algorithms.

![Comparison of evolutionary paradigms](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.01658v1/x2.png)
*Figure 1: Evolution of search paradigms from fixed evolutionary loops to autonomous single-agent and multi-agent evolution. While fixed search relies on external rules for selection and updates, CORAL grants agents the autonomy to decide how to retrieve information, propose candidates, and store collective knowledge.*

## The Limitations of Fixed Evolutionary Search

Open-ended discovery is characterized by problems where the goal is to continuously improve a solution, guided by an evaluator $E(x, y)$ that provides a score $s$ and qualitative feedback $f$. In conventional systems like FunSearch or AlphaEvolve, an LLM acts as a "mutation operator." It receives a set of high-performing parent programs selected by a pre-defined algorithm (such as MAP-Elites) and is tasked with generating a slightly different version.

While successful, this approach has a significant drawback: the search strategy is rigid. The decision of which parent solutions to inspect, when to run intermediate tests, and what specific insights to record for future attempts are all determined by external heuristics. These heuristics are often generic and may not be optimal for a specific, complex problem like kernel engineering. CORAL addresses this by treating the evolutionary loop itself as a task for the agent. Instead of being told which parents to use, the agent is given a workspace and the autonomy to explore the history of all attempts, decide its own strategy, and manage its own internal "knowledge base."

## The CORAL Architecture: Autonomous Multi-Agent Evolution

The CORAL framework is built around the concept of a population of $N$ agents working in parallel. Unlike "vertical scaling" systems, where agents are assigned specific roles (like a "manager" and a "coder"), CORAL agents are homogeneous. They collaborate "horizontally" through an indirect coordination mechanism.

At the heart of the system is the Shared Persistent Memory, implemented as a structured file system. Each agent $i$ has its own private workspace but can read from and write to the shared repository. This repository is organized into three primary directories:

1.  **`attempts/`**: A leaderboard of historical candidate solutions, their source code, and their evaluation outcomes.
2.  **`notes/`**: A collection of markdown files where agents record observations, reflections, and analyses of what worked and what failed.
3.  **`skills/`**: A library of reusable tools, scripts, and implementation patterns that agents have developed to automate parts of their search.

![System architecture of CORAL](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.01658v1/x3.png)
*Figure 2: The CORAL architecture. Multiple agents operate in private workspaces while interacting with a shared persistent memory. Heartbeat monitors periodically intervene to prompt reflection, consolidation of knowledge, or redirection if the search stagnates.*

This shared memory $M$ acts as a collective intelligence hub. When an agent $i$ writes a discovery $W_i^t$ to the memory, any other agent $j$ can retrieve that discovery as part of its context $M_j^t$ in a future iteration. This allows successful strategies to "diffuse" through the population naturally. If one agent discovers a specific loop-unrolling technique that improves performance, other agents can read the corresponding note, try the same technique on their own variations, and build upon it.

## Heartbeat Interventions and Strategic Steering

To ensure that autonomous agents remain productive over long horizons, CORAL introduces "heartbeat-based interventions." Because agents can sometimes get stuck in local minima (repeatedly trying variations of a failing strategy) or neglect to document their findings, the system manager periodically triggers structured prompts that force a modification to the agent's local context $C_i^t \to C_i^{t'}$.

There are three primary types of heartbeats:
*   **Reflection Heartbeats**: These occur at every iteration, prompting the agent to write a quick note about its current attempt. This captures immediate insights before they are lost in the next cycle.
*   **Consolidation Heartbeats**: These are triggered periodically to have the agents step back, review the collective notes, and merge overlapping findings into formal "skills."
*   **Redirection Heartbeats**: If an agent fails to improve its score after several attempts, this heartbeat activates. It prompts the agent to perform a "post-mortem" analysis of its recent failures and try a fundamentally different strategy, preventing the search from stagnating.

## Discovery in High-Stakes Domains

The researchers evaluated CORAL on two "stress-test" tasks that represent real-world discovery challenges: GPU kernel engineering and polyomino packing.

### Kernel Engineering
In kernel engineering, the goal is to optimize low-level GPU code to minimize execution cycles. This is a notoriously difficult task because performance depends on intricate hardware details like memory latency, register usage, and instruction scheduling. The primary metric is the speedup over a baseline:

$$\text{Score} = \frac{\text{Baseline Cycles}}{\text{Optimized Cycles}}$$

CORAL achieved a new state-of-the-art result on a standard benchmark, reducing the execution time from the previous best of 1,363 cycles down to 1,103 cycles. This 18.3% improvement was achieved by four agents working together to stack multiple optimization tricks—such as pipelining and data loading overlaps—that were discovered independently and then combined via the shared memory.

![Kernel engineering performance comparison](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.01658v1/x1.png)
*Figure 3: Performance of CORAL on the kernel engineering task. CORAL (4 agents) significantly outperforms both fixed evolutionary search (OpenEvolve) and its own single-agent variant, eventually surpassing the previous human-best known result.*

### Polyomino Packing
Polyomino packing is a geometric puzzle where agents must fit various shapes into a grid as tightly as possible. Unlike mathematical optimization, this requires spatial reasoning and the development of complex placement heuristics. CORAL agents successfully developed packing strategies that achieved 89.4% grid coverage, surpassing the previous best of 87%.

![Polyomino packing results](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.01658v1/x4.png)
*Figure 4: Polyomino packing results. The autonomous evolution of CORAL allows it to discover dense packing arrangements that exceed the capabilities of a single-shot LLM attempt and previous optimized search results.*

## Why Does Autonomous Evolution Work?

The success of CORAL is not just a result of using more compute; it stems from how the agents use that compute. The researchers identified three key reasons for its performance:

**1. Knowledge Accumulation and Reuse**
In fixed evolutionary loops, the "knowledge" of the system is contained only in the source code of the parent solutions. In CORAL, the notes and skills provide a high-level conceptual understanding of the problem. Agents were found to refer to other agents' notes in 87% of successful rounds in the polyomino task. By creating "skills" (such as a custom benchmarking script), agents could automate the validation of their ideas, leading to a much higher "improvement rate" per evaluation.

**2. Exploration Diversity**
Because each agent maintains its own strategy and workspace, they explore different parts of the solution space. An analysis of the strategies used by the agents showed low overlap, meaning the population was effectively diversifying its search. One agent might focus on memory optimization while another focuses on arithmetic simplification. When one succeeds, the others can adopt the successful part of that strategy without losing their own unique approach.

**3. Local Verification**
Autonomous agents tend to run their own local tests before submitting a solution for formal evaluation. On the kernel engineering task, agents performed local tests 57% of the time. This "internal monologue" and self-correction loop mean that the evaluations the system does perform are of much higher quality, making more efficient use of the limited evaluation budget.

## Conclusion

CORAL demonstrates that the next step in AI-driven discovery lies in moving from rigid, human-designed algorithms to autonomous, self-organizing agent systems. By providing agents with the tools to manage their own evolution—shared memory, the ability to create skills, and mechanisms for reflection—CORAL allows for a more flexible and powerful search process. This framework not only achieves new state-of-the-art results in technical domains like code optimization but also provides a template for how collective AI intelligence can be harnessed to solve open-ended problems where the path to a solution is not yet mapped.

keyInsights: Granting LLM agents autonomy over evolutionary decisions and facilitating local verification steps leads to substantially more efficient search and higher improvement rates compared to fixed evolutionary algorithms.

Asynchronous multi-agent organization with shared persistent memory fosters emergent collaboration and diverse exploration, extending the search frontier beyond what single autonomous agents can achieve.

The systematic accumulation and sharing of knowledge, via agent-generated notes and reusable skills, is critical for driving successful and sustained discovery, especially in complex open-ended tasks.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-powered evolutionary search relies on fixed heuristics and external rules, limiting agent autonomy and adaptability in complex, open-ended problem spaces where optimal search strategies are unknown.

Current multi-agent LLM systems often use 'vertical scaling' with human-defined roles and rigid communication, which is restrictive for truly open-ended discovery where optimal task decomposition and interaction patterns are not known in advance.
results: A single CORAL agent outperformed fixed evolutionary search baselines on all 11 mathematical and systems optimization tasks, achieving new state-of-the-art results on 8 tasks and demonstrating a 3-10x higher improvement rate.

Multi-agent CORAL (4 agents) extended search performance on challenging stress-test problems, achieving an 18.3% cycle reduction in kernel engineering (a new SOTA) and a 5.0% score increase in polyominoes packing.

Analysis revealed that multi-agent gains stem from cross-agent information transfer (e.g., 36% of attempts on Kernel Engineering used cross-agent parents with higher improvement rates) and increased exploration diversity (low Jaccard similarity of strategies across agents).
solution: CORAL implements autonomous multi-agent evolution by empowering LLM agents with control over the retrieve, propose, evaluate, and update stages of the evolutionary loop, rather than relying on fixed external rules.

A shared persistent memory, structured as a file system, enables asynchronous multi-agent organization where agents explore solution spaces in parallel and indirectly exchange discoveries, notes, and reusable skills.

Heartbeat-based interventions (e.g., periodic consolidation, stagnation redirection) are incorporated to prevent agents from getting stuck, encourage knowledge contribution, and dynamically reorient search strategies.
summary.summary: The CORAL framework introduces an autonomous multi-agent evolutionary system, empowering Large Language Model agents to control their discovery process and collaborate indirectly through shared persistent memory. This approach achieved new state-of-the-art results on 8 out of 11 mathematical and systems optimization tasks, exhibiting a 3-10x higher improvement rate and an 18.3% cycle reduction in kernel engineering.

title: CORAL: Towards Autonomous Multi-Agent Evolution for Open-Ended Discovery
